mnemonics.title: Cloud Service Models
mnemonics.content: I P S - Infrastructure (IaaS) comes first as it provides the foundation, Platform (PaaS) builds on it, Software (SaaS) is the complete product at the top. Think of building a house: IaaS provides land and basic structure, PaaS adds utilities and framework, SaaS is the fully furnished home.

mnemonics.title: Auto-Scaling Triggers
mnemonics.content: CPU MEM NET - Remember: Cloud Scales when CPU is High, MEMory is stressed, or NETwork is saturated. These are the main triggers for auto-scaling policies.

mnemonics.title: Cloud Benefits
mnemonics.content: E D C F - Elasticity, Disaster recovery, Cost optimization, Flexibility - These are the four key cloud advantages for capacity planning.

acronyms.usage: Provides virtual servers, storage, networking. Examples: AWS EC2, Google Compute Engine, Azure Virtual Machines
Provides development platform without managing infrastructure. Examples: Google App Engine, Azure App Service, Heroku
Software delivered over internet. Examples: Gmail, Salesforce, Microsoft 365
Cloud-based disaster recovery solution for business continuity
Maximum acceptable time to restore services after disruption
Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time

visualTips.visualization: Picture a building: Horizontal scaling = building more floors (more identical units); Vertical scaling = making each floor taller (bigger units). Horizontal is like cloning workers, vertical is giving each worker superpowers.
Imagine a bucket (private cloud) overflowing into a larger container (public cloud) during heavy rain. The overflow automatically transfers without the bucket overflowing.
Think of an elastic band - it stretches (scales up) when you pull (demand increases) and shrinks (scales down) when you release. The cloud does this automatically!
Like a taxi meter - you pay only for the distance traveled (resources used), no need to buy the entire car (hardware).

analogies.analogy: Like a utility company (electricity/water) - you pay for what you use, no need to build your own power plant. The cloud provides computing 'utilities' on demand.
Like a restaurant hiring more staff during lunch/dinner rush and sending them home when it's quiet - matching workforce to demand.
Like an airport air traffic control tower directing planes to different runways, ensuring no single runway is overwhelmed.
Like having accounts with multiple banks - if one has issues, you can use another, and each offers different benefits.
